mnemonics.title: Right to Left Rule
mnemonics.content: Remember: Default arguments must be placed from RIGHT to LEFT. Think of it like filling water in a container - it fills from bottom to top, similarly defaults go from rightmost to leftmost parameter.

mnemonics.title: Declaration First
mnemonics.content: D-E-F: Declaration Specifies Defaults. The function Declaration is where you specify the Default values, not the Definition.

mnemonics.title: Default Order
mnemonics.content: R-C-D: Rightmost parameter Comes Default first. The rightmost parameter gets its default first if arguments are missing.

acronyms.usage: Remember: Default arguments are specified in the function Declaration, not Definition.
Rule for positioning default arguments in function parameters.
Error that occurs when default arguments are specified in both declaration and definition.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a staircase where each step represents a parameter. You can only set defaults from the bottom step (rightmost) going up. You cannot skip a step to set a higher one.
Think of default arguments like optional toppings on a pizza. If you don't specify a topping (argument), you get the default. You fill in from the right side first.
Default arguments = 1 function with options. Overloading = multiple functions with different names. Default is like having one door with multiple keys, overloading is like having multiple doors.

analogies.analogy: It's like ordering a combo meal - you can order the full combo (all arguments), or just the burger (partial arguments) and get the default sides (default arguments).
It's like setting up a playlist on shuffle mode - you can only enable shuffle from the rightmost song going leftwards in your settings.

Default arguments are like a restaurant offering 'customize your burger' (one menu item, optional ingredients), while function overloading is like having separate menu items for different burger combinations.
